---
title: "GSS Mode Selection: Comprehensive Demographic Analysis"
author: "Resty Fufunan"
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    df_print: paged
---

## 1. Introduction

This document performs a rigorous analysis of **selection bias** in the GSS 2022 and 2024 waves. We examine whether demographic characteristics predict a respondent's likelihood of completing the survey via **Web** versus Face-to-Face/Phone.

The analysis proceeds in two stages: 1. **Comparative Diagnostics:** We assess the predictive power of each demographic variable using Information Criteria (AIC/BIC), Likelihood Ratio Tests, and Tjur's \$D\$2. **Optimal Model Selection:** We use stepwise algorithms to determine the most robust combination of predictors.

## 2. Setup and Data Preparation

```{r setup, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# --- Libraries ---
library(tidyverse)
library(haven)
library(tibble)
library(knitr)
library(broom)
library(gt)

set.seed(94305)

# Output directory for saved figures and tables
fig_dir <- "outputs/mode_weightvars_logit"
dir.create(fig_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# --- Load Data ---
if (!exists("gss_data")) {
  gss_data <- readRDS("gss_data_16to24_r3.rds") 
  print("Using existing or simulated 'gss_data'.")
}

# Load weights for merging consistency
mode_weights <- read_dta("gss20222024_modeweights.dta")

# --- Prepare Analysis Dataset ---
gss_analysis_data_base <- gss_data %>%
  filter(year %in% c(2022, 2024)) %>%
  left_join(mode_weights, by = join_by(id, year)) %>%
  mutate(
    # Outcome: 1 = Web, 0 = F2F/Phone
    mode_fctr = case_when(
      mode %in% c(1, 2) ~ 0, 
      mode == 4         ~ 1, 
      TRUE              ~ NA_real_
    ),
    # Controls
    year = factor(year, levels = c("2022", "2024")),
    agegrp = factor(case_when(
      age %in% 18:29 ~ "18-29",
      age %in% 30:39 ~ "30-39",
      age %in% 40:49 ~ "40-49",
      age %in% 50:64 ~ "50-64",
      age >= 65      ~ "65+",
      TRUE           ~ NA_character_ 
    ), levels = c("18-29", "30-39", "40-49", "50-64", "65+")),
    degree_cat = factor(case_when(
      degree %in% 0:2 ~ "Less than BA", 
      degree == 3     ~ "BA",            
      degree == 4     ~ "Graduate",      
      TRUE            ~ NA_character_
    ), levels = c("Less than BA", "BA", "Graduate")),
    marital_cat = factor(case_when(
      marital == 1      ~ "Married",
      marital %in% 2:5 ~ "Not Married", 
      TRUE             ~ NA_character_
    ), levels = c("Not Married", "Married")),
    racehisp = factor(case_when(
      hispanic == 1             ~ "Hispanic",
      hispanic > 1 & race == 1 ~ "White, Non-Hispanic",
      hispanic > 1 & race == 2 ~ "Black, Non-Hispanic",
      hispanic > 1 & race == 3 ~ "Other, Non-Hispanic",
      TRUE                      ~ NA_character_
    ), levels = c("White, Non-Hispanic", "Black, Non-Hispanic", "Hispanic", "Other, Non-Hispanic")),
    f_born = factor(case_when(
      born == 1 ~ "Born in US",
      born == 2 ~ "Born Elsewhere",
      TRUE      ~ NA_character_
    ), levels = c("Born in US", "Born Elsewhere")),
    f_sex = factor(case_when(
      sex == 1 ~ "Male",
      sex == 2 ~ "Female",
      TRUE     ~ NA_character_
    ), levels = c("Male", "Female")),
    f_region = factor(case_when(
      region %in% 1:2 ~ "northeast",
      region %in% 3:4 ~ "midwest",
      region %in% 5:7 ~ "south",
      region %in% 8:9 ~ "west",
      TRUE            ~ NA_character_
    ), levels = c("northeast", "midwest", "south", "west"))
  )

# Listwise deletion for consistent N
selection_data <- gss_analysis_data_base %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(mode_fctr) & !is.na(year) & !is.na(agegrp) &      
    !is.na(degree_cat) & !is.na(marital_cat) & !is.na(racehisp) &    
    !is.na(f_born) & !is.na(f_sex) & !is.na(f_region)       
  )
```

## 3. Analysis Loop

```{r master-loop}
# --- Helper Functions ---
calc_mcfadden <- function(m) { 1 - (m$deviance / m$null.deviance) }

calc_tjurs <- function(m, d) {
  preds <- predict(m, type = "response")
  mean(preds[m$y == 1]) - mean(preds[m$y == 0])
}

# --- Variable Definitions ---
weighting_vars <- c("marital_cat", "agegrp", "degree_cat", "racehisp", "f_born", "f_sex", "f_region")
all_weight_vars_formula <- paste(weighting_vars, collapse = " + ")
full_model_string <- paste("mode_fctr ~ year +", all_weight_vars_formula)

table1_list <- list()
table2_list <- list()

print("Running comparative model diagnostics...")

for (target_var in weighting_vars) {
  
  # --- 1. Fit Models ---
  # M1: Bivariate (Variable only)
  m1 <- glm(as.formula(paste("mode_fctr ~", target_var)), 
            family = binomial, data = selection_data)
  
  # M2: Controls Only (Everything BUT Variable)
  other_vars <- setdiff(weighting_vars, target_var)
  m2 <- glm(as.formula(paste("mode_fctr ~ year +", paste(other_vars, collapse=" + "))), 
            family = binomial, data = selection_data)
  
  # M3: Full Model (Variable + Everything)
  m3 <- glm(as.formula(full_model_string), 
            family = binomial, data = selection_data)
  
  # --- 2. Calculate Base Metrics ---
  r2_m1 <- calc_mcfadden(m1); r2_m2 <- calc_mcfadden(m2); r2_m3 <- calc_mcfadden(m3)
  tjur_m1 <- calc_tjurs(m1, selection_data); tjur_m2 <- calc_tjurs(m2, selection_data); tjur_m3 <- calc_tjurs(m3, selection_data)
  aic_m1 <- AIC(m1); bic_m1 <- BIC(m1)
  aic_m2 <- AIC(m2); bic_m2 <- BIC(m2)
  aic_m3 <- AIC(m3); bic_m3 <- BIC(m3)
  
  # --- 3. Comparison 1: M1 vs. M3 (Total Gain) ---
  lrt_1v3 <- anova(m1, m3, test = "Chisq")
  table1_list[[target_var]] <- data.frame(
    Variable = target_var,
    LRT_pval = lrt_1v3$`Pr(>Chi)`[2],
    AIC_M1 = aic_m1, AIC_M3 = aic_m3, AIC_Delta = aic_m3 - aic_m1,
    BIC_M1 = bic_m1, BIC_M3 = bic_m3, BIC_Delta = bic_m3 - bic_m1,
    R2_M1 = r2_m1, R2_M3 = r2_m3, R2_Ratio_M3_M1 = r2_m3 / r2_m1,
    Tjur_Gain = tjur_m3 - tjur_m1
  )

  # --- 4. Comparison 2: M2 vs. M3 (Marginal Gain) ---
  lrt_2v3 <- anova(m2, m3, test = "Chisq")
  table2_list[[target_var]] <- data.frame(
    Variable = target_var,
    LRT_pval = lrt_2v3$`Pr(>Chi)`[2],
    AIC_M2 = aic_m2, AIC_M3 = aic_m3, AIC_Delta = aic_m3 - aic_m2,
    BIC_M2 = bic_m2, BIC_M3 = bic_m3, BIC_Delta = bic_m3 - bic_m2,
    R2_M2 = r2_m2, R2_M3 = r2_m3, R2_Ratio_M3_M2 = r2_m3 / r2_m2,
    Tjur_Gain = tjur_m3 - tjur_m2
  )
}
```

## 4. Results

### Table 1: M1 (Bivariate) vs. M3 (Full)

*Comparison of the simple bivariate model against the full demographic model.*

```{r show-table-1}
final_table1 <- bind_rows(table1_list) %>%
  mutate(
    across(c(AIC_M1, AIC_M3, BIC_M1, BIC_M3, AIC_Delta, BIC_Delta), ~ round(., 1)),
    across(c(R2_M1, R2_M3, Tjur_Gain, R2_Ratio_M3_M1), ~ round(., 3)),
    Sig = case_when(LRT_pval < 0.001 ~ "***", LRT_pval < 0.01 ~ "**", LRT_pval < 0.05 ~ "*", TRUE ~ ""),
    LRT_pval = format.pval(LRT_pval, digits=3, eps=0.001)
  ) %>%
  select(Variable, AIC_M1, AIC_M3, AIC_Delta, BIC_M1, BIC_M3, BIC_Delta, R2_M1, R2_M3, R2_Ratio_M3_M1, Tjur_Gain, LRT_pval, Sig)

gt1 <- final_table1 %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "Table 1: Bivariate (M1) vs. Full Model (M3)") %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(AIC_M1, AIC_M3, AIC_Delta, BIC_M1, BIC_M3, BIC_Delta), decimals = 1) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(R2_M1, R2_M3, Tjur_Gain, R2_Ratio_M3_M1), decimals = 3) %>%
  cols_label(
    AIC_Delta = "\u0394AIC", BIC_Delta = "\u0394BIC",
    R2_M1 = "R\u00b2 M1", R2_M3 = "R\u00b2 M3",
    R2_Ratio_M3_M1 = "R\u00b2 Ratio", Tjur_Gain = "Tjur Gain",
    LRT_pval = "LRT p", Sig = ""
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = 11)

gt1

gtsave(gt1, file.path(fig_dir, "table1_m1_vs_m3.png"), expand = 10)
```

#### Interpretation

Table 1 tests the relative fit of simple demographic models against a comprehensive demographic profile. The results demonstrate that no single variable serves as a sufficient proxy for the mechanism of survey mode selection.

**1. Relative Improvement in Model Fit** Comparing the **McFaddenâ€™s Pseudo-**$R^2$ values reveals the limitations of bivariate proxies. The Full Model (M3) achieves a Pseudo-$R^2$ of 0.036. While this absolute value is modestâ€”indicating that unmeasured factors (such as personality or convenience) still play a large role in mode choiceâ€”it is a massive improvement over the bivariate models. For instance, **Education** (`degree_cat`) achieves a Pseudo-$R^2$ of only 0.017 on its own. The Full Model effectively doubles the explanatory power of Education alone, and captures over twelve times as much signal as Age alone ($R^2$ Ratio = 12.5).

**2. Hierarchy of Predictors** The **AIC and BIC Deltas** confirm a hierarchy of demographic sorting. **Marital Status** and **Education** provide the strongest individual starting points (lowest AIC/BIC values among M1 models). Conversely, **Sex** and **Region** show almost no improvement over a null model ($R^2 \approx 0$). This confirms that the decision to complete the survey online is not a function of geography or gender, but strongly tied to social and structural resources.

**3. The "Web Respondent" Profile** The fact that the Full Model yields such significant reductions in Deviance (AIC/BIC) compared to bivariate models suggests that the "Web Respondent" is defined by an intersection of traits. It is not simply that "educated people" choose the web; rather, the probability of web selection is highest when education intersects with specific household structures (marriage) and age cohorts.

### Table 2: M2 (Controls) vs. M3 (Full)

*Comparison of the "controls only" model against the Full Model.*

```{r show-table-2}
final_table2 <- bind_rows(table2_list) %>%
  mutate(
    across(c(AIC_M2, AIC_M3, BIC_M2, BIC_M3, AIC_Delta, BIC_Delta), ~ round(., 1)),
    across(c(R2_M2, R2_M3, Tjur_Gain, R2_Ratio_M3_M2), ~ round(., 3)),
    Sig = case_when(LRT_pval < 0.001 ~ "***", LRT_pval < 0.01 ~ "**", LRT_pval < 0.05 ~ "*", TRUE ~ ""),
    LRT_pval = format.pval(LRT_pval, digits=3, eps=0.001)
  ) %>%
  select(Variable, AIC_M2, AIC_M3, AIC_Delta, BIC_M2, BIC_M3, BIC_Delta, R2_M2, R2_M3, R2_Ratio_M3_M2, Tjur_Gain, LRT_pval, Sig) %>%
  arrange(BIC_Delta)

gt2 <- final_table2 %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "Table 2: Controls Only (M2) vs. Full Model (M3)") %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(AIC_M2, AIC_M3, AIC_Delta, BIC_M2, BIC_M3, BIC_Delta), decimals = 1) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = c(R2_M2, R2_M3, Tjur_Gain, R2_Ratio_M3_M2), decimals = 3) %>%
  cols_label(
    AIC_Delta = "\u0394AIC", BIC_Delta = "\u0394BIC",
    R2_M2 = "R\u00b2 M2", R2_M3 = "R\u00b2 M3",
    R2_Ratio_M3_M2 = "R\u00b2 Ratio", Tjur_Gain = "Tjur Gain",
    LRT_pval = "LRT p", Sig = ""
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = 11)

gt2

gtsave(gt2, file.path(fig_dir, "table2_m2_vs_m3.png"), expand = 10)
```

#### Interpretation

Table 2 identifies the **unique marginal contribution** of each demographic variable by testing if it improves model fit *after* all other controls are included. This distinguishes the primary drivers of selection from variables that are merely correlated with them.

**1. Primary Drivers: Education and Household Structure** **Education** (`degree_cat`) is the strongest independent driver of mode selection. Adding it to the model yields the largest drop in Information Criteria (BIC $\Delta = -88.6$) and the highest **Tjurâ€™s Coefficient of Discrimination** (Gain = 0.017). This indicates that "Education" captures a unique dimension of the selection mechanismâ€”likely literacy or comfort with text-based interfacesâ€”that is not explained by Age or Income. **Marital Status** (`marital_cat`) is the second most powerful independent predictor, suggesting that household dynamics (e.g., spousal gatekeeping or time usage) play a distinct role in whether a respondent chooses the self-administered web mode.

**2. Non-Predictive Factors** Crucially, **Sex** and **Region** fail to improve model fit ($p > .05$, Positive BIC Deltas). This means that any apparent differences in web participation by gender or region are likely spurious correlations driven by underlying differences in age or education. For weighting purposes, this implies that post-stratification on Sex and Region is less critical for correcting *mode* bias than it is for correcting general sampling error.

**3. The Efficiency of Race** While **Race/Hispanic** status is a statistically significant predictor ($p = .003$), its **Positive BIC Delta** (+12.0) suggests it is an inefficient predictor. Once we know a respondent's Education, Age, and Nativity status, their Race adds relatively little information about their likelihood of choosing the Web. This highlights that the "digital divide" in survey response is primarily socioeconomic rather than racial in nature.

## 5. Optimal Model Selection (Stepwise)

This section performs stepwise selection to identify the most efficient combination of predictors.

```{r stepwise-selection}

# Define Full Model
full_model_formula <- as.formula(paste("mode_fctr ~ year +", paste(weighting_vars, collapse = " + ")))
full_model <- glm(full_model_formula, family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = selection_data)

# --- 1. Stepwise AIC Selection ---
print("Running Stepwise AIC (k=2)...")
best_model_aic <- step(full_model, direction = "both", trace = 0, k = 2)

# --- 2. Stepwise BIC Selection ---
n_obs <- nrow(selection_data)
print(paste("Running Stepwise BIC (k=", round(log(n_obs), 2), ")..."))
best_model_bic <- step(full_model, direction = "both", trace = 0, k = log(n_obs))

# --- Results Summary ---
cat("\n=========================================\n")
cat("FINAL MODEL SELECTED BY AIC (Predictive)\n")
cat("=========================================\n")
print(formula(best_model_aic))
print(paste("AIC:", round(AIC(best_model_aic), 1)))

cat("\n=========================================\n")
cat("FINAL MODEL SELECTED BY BIC (Parsimonious)\n")
cat("=========================================\n")
print(formula(best_model_bic))
print(paste("BIC:", round(BIC(best_model_bic), 1)))
```

## 6. Dominance Analysis (Shapley Value Decomposition)

While step-wise selection identifies an efficient subset of predictors, it does not quantify the relative importance of correlated variables (e.g., Age and Education).

To address this, we perform a **Dominance Analysis** (Azen & Traxel, 2009). This method calculates the marginal contribution of each variable to the model fit (McFadden's $R^2$) averaged across all possible subset models ($2^k$). This provides a robust "variance partition" that handles multicollinearity, allowing us to rank predictors by their true explanatory power.

```{r dominance-analysis, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Load library
library(dominanceanalysis)

print("Running Dominance Analysis on Full Model...")

# 1. Define Full Model
full_model_formula <- as.formula(paste("mode_fctr ~ year +", paste(weighting_vars, collapse = " + ")))
full_model <- glm(full_model_formula, family = binomial(link = "logit"), data = selection_data)

# 2. Run Analysis (McFadden R2)
da_results <- dominanceAnalysis(full_model, target = "r2.m")

# 3. Extract General Dominance Statistics
# This averages the marginal contribution across all subset models
da_stats <- da_results$contribution.average$r2.m 

da_df <- data.frame(
  Variable = names(da_stats),
  Contribution = as.numeric(da_stats)
) %>%
  mutate(
    Rank = rank(-Contribution),
    Percent_of_Fit = (Contribution / sum(Contribution)) * 100
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Contribution))

# 4. Display Results
gt3 <- da_df %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "Table 3: General Dominance — Relative Importance of Predictors") %>%
  fmt_number(columns = Contribution, decimals = 4) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = Percent_of_Fit, decimals = 1) %>%
  fmt_integer(columns = Rank) %>%
  cols_label(
    Contribution   = "Shapley Contribution",
    Percent_of_Fit = "% of Fit",
    Rank           = "Rank"
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note("McFadden R\u00b2 averaged across all 2^k subset models.") %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = 11)

gt3

gtsave(gt3, file.path(fig_dir, "table3_dominance_analysis.png"), expand = 10)

# 5. Visualization
da_plot <- plot(da_results, which.graph = "general", fit.function = "r2.m") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Relative Importance of Predictors",
       subtitle = "Average Marginal Contribution to McFadden's R2",
       y = "Contribution to Model Fit",
       x = "") +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave(file.path(fig_dir, "figure1_dominance_plot.png"),
       plot = da_plot, width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 300)

da_plot
```

While previous steps identified *significant* predictors, the Dominance Analysis (Shapley Value Decomposition) provides a rigorous ranking of "importance" by quantifying the average marginal contribution of each variable to the model's overall explanatory power. This method resolves the ambiguity between correlated predictors (e.g., Age and Education) to reveal which factors truly drive the selection mechanism.

## 7. Combined Summary Table (Table 1)

This table merges the logistic regression odds ratios from the full model with the Shapley general dominance statistics, producing the summary table referenced in the manuscript. Odds ratios and confidence intervals are reported at the **variable level** by taking the geometric mean across all dummy contrasts within a factor, with the range of contrasts forming the CI bounds displayed.

```{r table1-combined, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
library(broom)

# --- 1. Extract full-model coefficients ---
# full_model is already fitted above (line ~240)
or_df <- tidy(full_model, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.int = TRUE) %>%
  filter(term != "(Intercept)") %>%
  # Map each coefficient term back to its parent variable
  mutate(
    variable = case_when(
      str_starts(term, "degree_cat")  ~ "degree_cat",
      str_starts(term, "marital_cat") ~ "marital_cat",
      str_starts(term, "agegrp")      ~ "agegrp",
      str_starts(term, "year")        ~ "year",
      str_starts(term, "racehisp")    ~ "racehisp",
      str_starts(term, "f_born")      ~ "f_born",
      str_starts(term, "f_region")    ~ "f_region",
      str_starts(term, "f_sex")       ~ "f_sex",
      TRUE ~ term
    )
  )

# Collapse to variable level:
# OR = geometric mean of exponentiated coefficients within variable
# CI = [min(conf.low), max(conf.high)] across contrasts within variable
or_summary <- or_df %>%
  group_by(variable) %>%
  summarise(
    OR       = exp(mean(log(estimate))),
    CI_low   = min(conf.low),
    CI_high  = max(conf.high),
    .groups  = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    `95% CI` = paste0(
      "[", formatC(CI_low,  digits = 2, format = "f"), ", ",
           formatC(CI_high, digits = 2, format = "f"), "]"
    )
  )

# --- 2. Join with Shapley dominance results ---
# da_df has columns: Variable, Contribution, Rank, Percent_of_Fit
table1_combined <- da_df %>%
  rename(variable = Variable) %>%
  left_join(or_summary, by = "variable") %>%
  arrange(Rank) %>%
  transmute(
    Variable                  = variable,
    `Odds Ratio`              = round(OR, 3),
    `95% CI`                  = `95% CI`,
    `Shapley Contribution`    = round(Contribution, 4),
    `% of Fit`                = round(Percent_of_Fit, 1)
  )

gt4 <- table1_combined %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title    = "Table 1: Predictors of Web vs. FTF/Phone Completion, 2022\u20132024",
    subtitle = "Logistic regression odds ratios and Shapley general dominance statistics"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = `Odds Ratio`, decimals = 3) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = `Shapley Contribution`, decimals = 4) %>%
  fmt_number(columns = `% of Fit`, decimals = 1) %>%
  cols_label(
    Variable               = "Variable",
    `Odds Ratio`           = "Odds Ratio",
    `95% CI`               = "95% CI",
    `Shapley Contribution` = "Shapley Contribution",
    `% of Fit`             = "% of Fit"
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "Logistic Regression",
    columns = c(`Odds Ratio`, `95% CI`)
  ) %>%
  tab_spanner(
    label   = "Dominance Analysis",
    columns = c(`Shapley Contribution`, `% of Fit`)
  ) %>%
  tab_source_note(
    "OR = geometric mean of dummy-contrast odds ratios within variable. 95% CI = [min, max] across contrasts. Shapley contribution = average marginal McFadden R\u00b2 across all 2^k subset models."
  ) %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = 11) %>%
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_spanners()
  )

gt4

gtsave(gt4, file.path(fig_dir, "table4_combined_or_shapley.png"), expand = 10)
```

**1. The Dominance of Socioeconomic Status** **Education** (`degree_cat`) consistently emerges as the primary determinant of survey mode selection. In the General Dominance ranking, it accounts for the largest share of the modelâ€™s total fit (typically 30-40% of the explainable variance). This indicates that the "digital divide" in the GSS is fundamentally a function of human capitalâ€”likely serving as a proxy for digital literacy, reading comprehension comfort, and access to high-speed internet. It is the single most important variable to control for when attempting to balance the sample.

**2. The "Life Cycle" Cluster** **Marital Status** (`marital_cat`) and **Age** (`agegrp`) form a secondary tier of importance. The analysis shows that these variables carry significant weight independent of education. This suggests that mode selection is also driven by **life course availability**: \* **Marital Status** likely captures household dynamics (e.g., time constraints or "gatekeeper" effects) that make self-administration (Web) more or less attractive than interacting with an interviewer. \* **Age** captures the generational cohort effects of technology adoption, though its lower rank compared to Education suggests that "older but educated" respondents are more likely to participate online than "younger but less educated" ones.

**3. Factors with Minimal Explanatory Power** Crucially, **Sex**, **Region**, and **Nativity** rank at the bottom of the dominance hierarchy, contributing minimal unique information to the model. This confirms that observed differences in web participation across genders or regions are almost entirely compositional (e.g., "Web respondents in the Northeast are higher because they are more educated," not because of something intrinsic to the Northeast).

**Conclusion: The "Variance Partition"** The Dominance Analysis clarifies that the mechanism of selection into the Web mode is not random nor broadly demographic. It is highly specific to **resource-rich** (high education) and **socially structured** (married) households. Consequently, post-stratification weights that prioritize accurate adjustment of Education and Marital Status will be far more effective at reducing mode bias than those focusing on Geography or Sex.
